title: Future perfect continuous: emphasis on duration
question: By the time she retires, she _____ at that hospital for 35 years.
explanation: The future perfect continuous is formed with 'will have been + -ing'. The distractors all mangle this auxiliary chain ('been worked', 'have being', missing 'have').

title: Future perfect continuous form
question: By midnight, they _____ for twelve hours straight.
explanation: The correct form is 'will have been + present participle'. '*will have been paint' uses a bare infinitive, '*will been' drops 'have', and '*will have be painting' uses 'be' instead of 'been'.

title: Future perfect: completed quantity by a future point
question: By the end of the tour, the band _____ over 80 concerts.
explanation: The future perfect is 'will have + past participle'. '*will have play' uses a bare infinitive, '*will has' wrongly inflects the auxiliary, and '*will been played' drops 'have' and turns the verb passive.

title: Narrative past perfect: form
question: The streets were wet. It _____ heavily during the night.
explanation: The past perfect is 'had + past participle'. '*had rain' lacks the participle, '*had been rain' is malformed, and '*was rained' turns an intransitive verb passive, which is impossible.

title: Past perfect continuous: form
question: His eyes were red because he _____ at the screen all day.
explanation: The past perfect continuous is 'had been + -ing'. '*had been stare' drops the -ing, '*had being' uses the wrong form of 'be', and '*was been' is ungrammatical.

title: Present perfect continuous: form
question: I _____ this book for three weeks and I'm only halfway through.
explanation: Present perfect continuous is 'have/has been + -ing'. '*have been read' is passive and wrong with an active object, '*has been' disagrees with 'I', and '*am been' is ungrammatical.

title: Past perfect continuous: interrupted duration
question: They _____ for an hour when the fire alarm went off.
explanation: The past perfect continuous is 'had been + -ing'. '*had been wait' omits the -ing, '*had being waited' uses the wrong participle form, and '*were been' is ungrammatical.

title: Future perfect vs future continuous: meaning
question: This time next year, I _____ my degree, so I'll be free to travel.
explanation: The sentence requires a completed action before a future point — future perfect 'will have + past participle'. '*will have completing', '*will has', and '*will be completed' (passive, no agent-friendly reading here) are all grammatically wrong forms.

title: Tense in time clauses: no future after 'as soon as'
question: I'll call you as soon as I _____ at the hotel.
explanation: After 'as soon as', 'when', 'before', 'after', and 'until', English uses the present, not 'will'. '*will arrive', '*will be arrive', and '*am arrived' are all ungrammatical in a time clause.

title: Future continuous: form
question: Don't call at 8 — we _____ dinner then.
explanation: Future continuous is 'will be + -ing'. '*will be have' drops the -ing, '*will have been' is future perfect (no main verb here), and '*will being have' is ungrammatical.

title: Future perfect passive: form
question: By Friday, the report _____ to every department.
explanation: Future perfect passive is 'will have been + past participle'. The other three scramble the auxiliary order in ways that are ungrammatical in any context.

title: Past perfect: sequencing
question: When I got to the station, the train _____.
explanation: The earlier action takes past perfect: 'had + past participle'. '*had already leave' uses a bare infinitive, '*had already leaving' uses a present participle where a past participle is required, and '*leaved' is not a word (the past participle of 'leave' is 'left').

title: Nevertheless: which sentence uses it correctly?
question: Which sentence uses 'nevertheless' in a grammatically well-formed way?
correct: The results were disappointing; nevertheless, the team decided to continue.
wrong1: The results were disappointing nevertheless the team decided to continue.
wrong2: The results were disappointing, nevertheless team decided to continue.
wrong3: The results were disappointing; nevertheless the team to continue decided.
explanation: 'Nevertheless' is a sentence adverb that needs proper punctuation (semicolon or new sentence) and a well-formed clause after it. The other options have run-on punctuation, a missing article, or scrambled word order.

title: Discourse marker at clause-final position
question: Which sentence places 'nonetheless' in a grammatically acceptable position?
correct: The evidence was weak. She pressed ahead with the claim nonetheless.
wrong1: The evidence was weak. She nonetheless pressed the claim with ahead.
wrong2: The evidence was weak. She ahead with the claim pressed nonetheless.
wrong3: The evidence was weak. She the claim nonetheless pressed ahead with.
explanation: 'Nonetheless' can appear at the end of a clause. The other three contain word-order errors that are ungrammatical in any context.

title: Moreover: form and placement
question: Which sentence is grammatically well-formed?
correct: The restaurant was the best in town. Moreover, it was the cheapest.
wrong1: The restaurant was the best in town. Moreover it the cheapest was.
wrong2: The restaurant the best in town was. Moreover, was it the cheapest.
wrong3: The restaurant was best town in the. Moreover, cheapest was it the.
explanation: Only the first sentence has correct English word order and article placement. The other three contain structural errors in every context.

title: In contrast: linking parallel data
question: Which sentence is grammatically well-formed?
correct: Male participants improved by 40%. In contrast, female participants showed no change.
wrong1: Male participants improved by 40%. In contrast, female participants no change showed.
wrong2: Male participants by 40% improved. In contrast, showed female participants no change.
wrong3: Male participants improved 40% by. In contrast, no change female participants showed.
explanation: Only the first sentence has standard English word order. The other three contain verb-object inversions and misplaced prepositions that are ungrammatical in any context.

title: Consequently: form of the following clause
question: Funding was cut by 40%. Consequently, three research projects _____.
explanation: After 'consequently', a well-formed clause is required. Subject-verb agreement ('three projects were'), correct participle ('suspended'), and a finite verb are all needed. The distractors each violate one of these.

title: Indeed: intensifying the previous statement
question: The experiment confirmed our hypothesis. Indeed, the effect _____ even stronger than predicted.
explanation: 'Indeed' must be followed by a well-formed clause. 'The effect was' takes a singular verb; '*were', '*been', and '*being' are all ungrammatical as the main verb of this clause.

title: Collocation: make + decision
question: After weeks of deliberation, they finally _____ a decision.
explanation: English uses 'make a decision'. 'Did a decision' is not a standard collocation, and '*maked' and '*doed' are not English verb forms.

title: Collocation: make + effort
question: The team _____ every effort to meet the client's deadline, even working weekends.
explanation: 'Make an effort' is a fixed collocation. 'Did/took/put an effort' are not standard English collocations, though 'put in effort' (with 'in') would be acceptable.

title: Collocation: do + damage
question: The storm _____ considerable damage to the coastline.
explanation: 'Do damage' is the standard collocation. '*doed' is not a word, '*done' without an auxiliary cannot be a finite past verb, and '*was done' would require a passive construction with a different subject.

title: Collocation: heavy + rain
question: We had to cancel the match because of the _____ rain.
explanation: 'Heavy rain' is the fixed collocation. '*heavies' is not an adjective, '*heavily' is an adverb (cannot modify a noun), and '*heavyer' is not a valid comparative form.

title: Collocation: heavy + traffic
question: Despite leaving early, she was stuck in _____ traffic for over two hours and missed her flight.
explanation: 'Heavy traffic' is a fixed collocation. '*heavily' is an adverb and cannot modify a noun directly, '*heavier' needs 'than' for a comparison, and '*the heavy' conflicts with the uncountable use here.

title: Collocation: great + achievement
question: Winning the scholarship was a _____ achievement for her.
explanation: 'A great achievement' is the standard collocation. '*greatly' is an adverb, '*greater than' needs a comparison, and 'the great' would require definite reference, not the existential 'was a'.

title: Collocation: large + amount
question: The project required a _____ amount of funding.
explanation: 'A large amount' needs a bare adjective. '*largely' is an adverb (wrong part of speech before a noun), '*the large' conflicts with the indefinite 'a', and '*much large' is ungrammatical.

title: Collocation: heavy + smoker
question: Her doctor warned her that being a _____ smoker would shorten her life.
explanation: 'Heavy smoker' is a fixed collocation. '*heavily' is an adverb and cannot modify a noun, and '*heavies' and '*heavyly' are not English words.

title: Collocation: make + progress
question: The patient has _____ remarkable progress since the surgery.
explanation: 'Has made progress' is the collocation and requires a past participle after 'has'. '*maked' is not a word, and bare '*make' or '*making' cannot follow 'has' as the main verb here.

title: Lay vs lie: present participle of transitive 'lay'
question: The workers spent the morning _____ new carpet throughout the office.
explanation: The present participle of transitive 'lay' (to put down) is 'laying'. 'Lying' is the participle of intransitive 'lie' (to recline) and cannot take an object. '*laining' and '*layed' are not English words.

title: Lay vs lie: past of intransitive 'lie'
question: She felt dizzy, so she _____ down on the sofa for an hour.
explanation: The past tense of intransitive 'lie' (to recline) is 'lay'. '*lainn', '*lieing', and '*lyed' are not English words.

title: Lay vs lie: past participle of 'lie'
question: He had _____ in bed all morning before anyone found him.
explanation: The past participle of intransitive 'lie' is 'lain'. 'Had lay' is ungrammatical (past tense cannot follow 'had'), and '*lainned' and '*lien' are not English words.

title: Lay vs lie: past of transitive 'lay'
question: She _____ the baby gently in the crib and tiptoed out.
explanation: 'Laid' is the past tense of transitive 'lay' (to put down). 'Lay' is the present/base form, not the past tense. '*lainned' and '*layed' are not English words.

title: Lay vs lie: present participle of 'lie'
question: The cat has been _____ on my laptop all afternoon.
explanation: The present participle of intransitive 'lie' is 'lying'. '*lieing', '*lainning', and '*lien' are not English words.

title: Affect vs effect: verb
question: The new regulations will _____ thousands of small businesses.
explanation: The verb meaning 'to influence' is 'affect'. '*effects' is a noun (and plural, so it can't follow 'will'), '*affection' is an unrelated noun, and '*affected' is a past form that cannot follow the modal 'will'.

title: Effect as a verb: form
question: The new CEO vowed to _____ sweeping changes within her first year.
explanation: After 'to', English requires the bare infinitive. '*effects' is a third-person singular form, '*effected' is a past form, and '*effecting' is a participle — none can follow 'to'.

title: Affect as a noun: form
question: The patient displayed a flat _____, showing no emotional response.
explanation: The noun slot after 'a flat' requires a singular noun. '*affects' is plural, '*affected' is an adjective/past participle, and '*affecting' is a participle — none work as a count noun after 'a'.

title: Effects: plural noun subject
question: The _____ of sleep deprivation on memory are well documented.
explanation: A plural subject is required to agree with 'are'. '*effect' is singular, and '*effecting' and '*affected' are not nouns that can head this subject.

title: Fewer vs less: countable plural
question: There are _____ students enrolled this year than last year.
explanation: 'Fewer' is used with countable plural nouns in comparative constructions. '*fewest' is a superlative (requires 'the'), and '*less of' and '*fewer of' require a determiner after 'of' ('less of the students').

title: Less with distance
question: The airport is _____ than ten miles from here.
explanation: 'Less' is used with measurements of distance even when technically countable. '*littler' is a nonstandard comparative, '*lesser' means 'smaller in importance', not quantity, and '*leaster' is not a word.

title: Less with sums of money
question: The repair cost _____ than £500.
explanation: A total sum of money takes 'less'. '*littler', '*leaster', and '*lesslier' are not valid English comparatives.

title: Fewer with countable people
question: _____ than fifty delegates attended the second day of the conference.
explanation: A comparative with countable plural nouns uses 'fewer'. '*fewest' is a superlative, '*fewerer' is not a word, and '*few than' is ungrammatical.

title: Lend: direction and form
question: Could you _____ me your umbrella until tomorrow?
explanation: The request needs the bare infinitive after 'could you'. '*lended' is not a word (past of lend is 'lent'), '*lent' is a past form that cannot follow 'could', and '*lending' is a participle.

title: Borrow: form after modal
question: Can I _____ your charger for a few minutes?
explanation: After the modal 'can', English requires the bare infinitive. '*borrowed', '*borrowing', and '*borrows' are all finite or participial forms and cannot follow 'can'.

title: Owe: present tense form
question: You paid for my lunch, so now I _____ you £15.
explanation: First-person present requires the bare form 'owe'. '*owes' is third-person (wrong agreement with 'I'), '*owing' is a participle and cannot be a finite verb, and '*oweing' is a nonexistent spelling.

title: Lend: past tense (irregular)
question: She _____ him her car last weekend.
explanation: The past of 'lend' is 'lent'. '*lended', '*lended out', and '*lendt' are not English words or forms.

title: Reported speech: pronoun + past perfect form
question: She told me she _____ the email before the meeting started.
explanation: Past perfect is 'had + past participle'. '*had sended' uses a nonexistent form, '*had been send' omits the participle, and '*was been sent' is ungrammatical.

title: Third conditional: form of the if-clause
question: If they _____ earlier, they would have caught the train.
explanation: The third conditional if-clause is 'had + past participle'. '*had leaved' is a nonexistent form, '*would had' is ungrammatical, and '*had leaving' uses a bare present participle where a past participle is required.

title: Mixed conditional: past condition, present result
question: If I _____ that job in Tokyo, I would be living in Japan now.
explanation: The past condition in a mixed conditional takes past perfect: 'had + past participle'. '*had took', '*would had taken', and '*had been took' all use nonexistent or ungrammatical forms.

title: Discourse: that said — form
question: The hotel was overpriced. _____, the location was unbeatable.
explanation: 'That said' is a fixed discourse marker. '*That saying', '*That been said', and '*That say' are not valid forms of this expression.

title: Discourse: thereby + participle
question: She moved production in-house, _____ cutting costs by 20%.
explanation: 'Thereby' attaches directly to a present participle ('cutting'). '*thereby she' adds a redundant subject, '*thereby was' inserts a finite verb, and '*thereby to' introduces an infinitive where the structure requires -ing.

title: Collocation: do + justice
question: The photograph doesn't _____ justice to the actual landscape.
explanation: After the auxiliary 'doesn't', English requires the bare infinitive 'do'. '*does', '*doing', and '*doed' cannot follow 'doesn't'.
